images.caption: Figure 1:LLMs admit a countable and practically finite number of prompts𝒱≤K\mathcal{V}^{\leq K}. This property implies the existence ofholesin their real activation spaceℝd\mathbb{R}^{d}: regions that do not map back to any prompt. We show thatactivation steering, a popular white-box intervention method to change model behavior, almost surely steers activations into suchholesresulting in almost-sure non-surjectivity, i.e.,steered model behavior is not exhibited by any real prompt.Details in §4.

Figure 2:Due to their almost sure injectivity,
natural LLM activations can uniquely recover prompts using the SipIt algorithm (§5.1).

Figure 3:We test the surjectivity of steered activations using two methods.(Left, §5.1)First, we collect steered activations𝐫~𝐬\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{s}}and useSipItto try and invert
them butfind no match(distance≫0\gg 0). We project𝐫~𝐬\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{s}}to the nearest tokens for a candidate𝐬′\mathbf{s}^{\prime}, butit does not generate steered activations/responses.(Right, §5.2)We try ICL prefixes for candidate𝐬′\mathbf{s}^{\prime}, but still findno alignment at the prompt(‖𝐫~𝐬−𝐫𝐬′‖≫0\|{\color[rgb]{0.00390625,0.5390625,0.109375}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0.00390625,0.5390625,0.109375}\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{\mathbf{s}}}-{\color[rgb]{0.7421875,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0.7421875,0,0}\mathbf{r}^{\prime}_{\mathbf{s}}}\|\gg 0)or response(‖𝐫~𝐠~−𝐫𝐠~′‖≫0\|{\color[rgb]{0.30859375,0.30078125,1}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0.30859375,0.30078125,1}\tilde{\mathbf{r}}_{\tilde{\mathbf{g}}}}-{\color[rgb]{0.64453125,0.0078125,0.71484375}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0.64453125,0.0078125,0.71484375}\mathbf{r}^{\prime}_{\tilde{\mathbf{g}}}}{\color[rgb]{0,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}\pgfsys@color@gray@stroke{0}\pgfsys@color@gray@fill{0}\|\gg 0})locations.

Figure 4:We sort the L2 distances between activations produced by all vocabulary tokens at each position with 1) the ground-truth natural activations (𝐫\mathbf{r}) and 2) steered activations (𝐫~\tilde{\mathbf{r}}). While natural activations recover the prompt exactly viaSipIt(L2≈0\approx 0for the top token), steered activations remain far from the activations of any natural token (top row). When forced to pick the nearest tokens creating a lossy reconstructed prompt (𝐬′\mathbf{s}^{\prime}), we find that it recovers the original test prompt (𝐬′≈𝐬\mathbf{s}^{\prime}\approx\mathbf{s},bottom row). This shows thatsteered activations do not correspond to other real prompts.

Figure 5:We plot the L2 distance between steered activations and model’s natural activations with ICL prefixes for various shot-countsNN. AsNNincreases, even with increasing attack success rates, the activations stray farther. This implies a different functional mechanism of the two attack methods.

header: Appendix DExperiment Details and Additional Results

images.caption: Figure 6:SipItexperiments on the Gemma model (first two plots) and Llama 3.2-1B-Instruct INT4 quanitized model (right most plot) shows similar trends. Gemma activations have large absolute values which scales the numbers. We did not perform a coefficient sweep for this model due to resource constraints.
Figure 7:SipItexperiments on the Qwen model show similar results.

Figure 8:ICL experiments on the Qwen, Gemma and even the quantized Llama (INT4) models have similar takeaway messages: None of the ICL prefix based natural activations come close to match the steered activations (L2≫0\gg 0); and Average L2 distances follow a non-decreasing trend (although the increase is rather flat).
